chapters.title: Reason, science, humanism, and progress can make life better
chapters.text:
Many people misunderstand the state of the world and assume it is falling apart. They picture a planet full of poverty, broken schools, and crime. Steven Pinker offers a different view, grounded in the ideals of the Enlightenment. Those ideals are reason, science, humanism, and progress, and they have driven the rise in health, wealth, safety, peace, and happiness around the world. During a public talk, a student asked Pinker what gives life meaning. She was not in despair or being ironic. She genuinely wanted an answer. Pinker pointed to the Enlightenment. Its core idea is that reason and sympathy can make human life better. Defending those ideals matters more than ever today.

The world keeps improving in ways the media rarely mentions. People live longer and healthier lives on average than at any point in history.

Plenty of good news goes unnoticed. The world is becoming more equal, and new technology is lifting poor people out of hardship. More people than ever are pushing for equal rights, something that would have been unthinkable a few centuries ago.

This summary walks through the ideals of the Enlightenment and how to defend them in the 21st century. It also covers the progress happening all around you that never makes the headlines.

The ideals of the Enlightenment grew out of human reason, but they always compete with other parts of human nature. Loyalty to tribe, obedience to authority, magical thinking, and blaming misfortune on evildoers all pull against them.

Steven Pinker

chapters.title: Enlightenment explains why we are alive
chapters.text:
Philosopher Immanuel Kant defined enlightenment as humanity's escape from the immaturity of selfishness and from blind submission to religious and political authority. He placed freedom of thought and speech at its heart, and he gave it the motto "dare to understand." Real progress depends on daring to understand science, morality, politics, and everything else. Problems never go away, because failure is just a sign of missing knowledge. Whenever knowledge runs short, problems appear. The path forward is the search for knowledge and the willingness to understand.

We only move forward when we think hard and put in the effort to understand the world around us.

When people fail, it is usually because they lacked the knowledge to win. That failure then turns into a problem, since the knowledge needed to fix it is also missing. Problems only show up when knowledge is incomplete. Enlightenment is a constant search for a better grasp of the human condition. Its four main themes are reason, science, humanism, and progress.

Reason urges us to apply logical thinking to the world, instead of accepting dogma, mysticism, fortune-telling, sacred texts, or appeals to authority without question. Science gives us an escape from ignorance and superstition by setting an empirical standard for reliable knowledge, including knowledge of ourselves. Humanism puts the well-being of individual men, women, and children above the glory of tribe, race, nation, or religion. Progress means using a scientific view of the world and a wider circle of sympathy, both driven by reason and humanity, to keep advancing intellectually and morally.

Enlightenment hopes for progress through institutions rather than through reshaping human nature itself.

The common sense of the 18th century, with its grasp of human suffering and human nature, washed over the world like a cleansing bath of morality.

Alfred North Whitehead

chapters.title: Entropy, evolution, and information are the three keys to understanding life
chapters.text:
Entropy means disorder, and it is the first key to understanding the human condition. Human life and pleasure depend on small pockets of order within a vast field of possibilities. When change happens that we did not start, we expect it to make things worse. One of our basic tasks is to use energy and knowledge to push back against natural entropy and build a livable order.

Disorder is everywhere, and our job is to fight it by creating controlled environments where we can thrive.

Evolution is the second key. For most of history, Enlightenment thinkers were deists rather than atheists, because nature looked like the work of a careful designer. The idea of a creator seemed necessary. Then naturalists like Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace made the creator unnecessary by proving evolution. Evolution shows that one purpose of life is to adapt and to get past the obstacles in our way, so that we survive. If we cannot reshape our surroundings, we can reshape ourselves to be stronger than the environment.

Information is the third key. It separates orderly, structured systems from the random, useless ones found in nature. Information makes evolution possible. Living things gather information during their lives and pass it on through the genome, where it shapes the next generation and helps it adapt. Information is one of the reasons we exist. We live to learn, not only for our own use, but for the lives that follow. We are carriers of information for the world around us and for what comes after us.

Entropy, evolution, and information together define the story of human progress. They describe the natural tragedy we were born into and the ways we build a better existence.

chapters.title: We should be grateful for our progress instead of ignoring it
chapters.text:
There is still much we do not know, much we have not done, and much we still fail at as a species. But the truth is that humans have come a long way. The typical intellectual enjoys the fruits of progress, like flying across the world instead of traveling for weeks on a horse or camel, or sending a text message instead of waiting months for a letter to arrive. What that same intellectual tends to reject is the idea of progress itself. Enlightenment thinkers get under their skin when they claim that understanding the world improves the human condition. They dismiss Enlightenment views as "blind faith" or a "quasi-religious belief."

Many intellectuals happily use the benefits of human progress, such as modern medicine and fast travel. Yet they refuse to admit that, even with much left to do, we have already accomplished a great deal. Progress, no matter how small, deserves respect.

If you only see negativity in the world, you will never grow.

The media reports more bad news than good news, but that is not because good news is scarce. It is because our intellectual culture has a negativity bias. We are trained to watch for threats, and the information market answers by pointing out every bad thing we may have missed. Bad things do happen, but there is no reason to ignore the good entirely. You cannot grow while staring only at the bad. That bias does not match reality. Within the constant pull of entropy, we are here to gather information and to evolve. That is the Enlightenment view. People who see the world as a lost cause rarely act to save it.

chapters.title: The world today shows both good and bad progress, and our choices decide the future
chapters.text:
The world is far from where it was when Enlightenment thinking emerged in the late 1700s. Compared with two centuries ago, average life expectancy has climbed from 30 to 71, according to work by Cunningham and colleagues in 2017. The world is more peaceful, with fewer people dying in wars and fewer wars breaking out, both within and between countries. According to a 2013 article by Max Roser and Esteban Ortiz-Ospina, literacy has spread widely. In the early 1800s, only 12 percent of the world could read and write. Today, that figure is 86 percent.

Stop focusing on what the media feeds you. Look around and notice the good that surrounds you.

These studies show exactly how much the Enlightenment has helped us grow. Some recent trends are less encouraging. About a dozen wars are raging around the world, and even peaceful countries hold thousands of nuclear weapons on standby. Around 5 million people die in accidents each year, and more than 400,000 are murdered. Global temperatures keep rising as tons of carbon dioxide, sulfur dioxide, and other pollutants pour into the atmosphere. Marine life has dropped by nearly 40 percent, and thousands of species are at risk of extinction.

These are real problems, and although the media gives them far more attention than the good news, they are not as overwhelming as they seem. We have already come so far that solving these problems is only a matter of time. After that, the path forward is our choice. We can keep dwelling on the negative, as our culture encourages, or we can focus on what is working and get inspired to fix what is broken. That second path is what carries humanity to the next step.

chapters.title: Equal rights come from Enlightenment and show how reason improves the world
chapters.text:
The push for equal rights has been underway for centuries, but only recently has it become truly mainstream. Equal rights means freedom from racism and from misogyny. Today, all people, regardless of race or gender, have access to the same opportunities. This shift has led to more educated people and fewer people in poverty. It has improved the world overall, because now everyone starts on a more level playing field and we work together on shared goals instead of wasting energy on oppression. It was reason and understanding that led to the conclusion that no one should be held back and everyone deserves a fair chance.

Enlightenment opened people's eyes and moved them away from old, cruel habits toward support for equal rights.

Progress on equal rights shows up in the data. For instance, research by Deaton in 2013 found that poverty among African Americans fell from 55 percent in 1960 to 27.6 percent in 2011. According to the United States Department of Labor in 2016, women made up 47 percent of the labor force and the majority of university students. Racism and misogyny still exist, and they should not, but we have come a long way from how bad things used to be. This progress shows that we can do even better.

Children around the world are better off than ever. They are less likely to enter the world without a mother, to die before age five, or to grow up stunted from lack of food.

Steven Pinker

A 2020 study by the Pew Research Center found that 77 percent of American adults called sexual harassment a major obstacle to women's equal rights.

chapters.title: Reason, science, and humanism as the pillars of Enlightenment
chapters.text:
Reason is not something we believe in. It is something we use. Many people resist reason, choosing feeling over thinking. They claim that reason is just a pretext for the exercise of power, and that what counts as reality is socially constructed. People try to push reason out of the Enlightenment, but it cannot be removed. Its importance to the human condition is undeniable. We must keep fighting for the Enlightenment ideal of finding reason and truth. Reason lets us step back, see our flaws, and find ways around them.

Reason asks people to think and to find new ways of doing things, or to understand why old ways were broken.

Science is one of humanity's proudest achievements. We have explained the history of the universe, the forces that govern it, what we are made of, how we came to be, and the workings of our bodies and minds. We have taken things apart, studied them, and understood them. There is still a lot we do not know, but the knowledge we have built is remarkable. Science keeps growing, filling in the gaps in our picture of reality. Science is the road to the truth that reason seeks. It is where the tests are run and the conclusions are drawn. From those conclusions, we can reason our way toward answers. Science is the spirit of the Enlightenment.

Humanism is the final ideal that ties the others together. Reason gives us thought, but thought can also be cruel. Science gave us understanding, but that understanding has also produced terrible weapons. Humanism centers the welfare of human beings and the world around them. It guides reason so that our thoughts lean toward the good rather than the evil. It keeps us from using knowledge without restraint and helps us stay on a good path. We will never build a perfect world, and chasing one would be dangerous. But there is no limit to the good we can reach if we keep applying knowledge to improve human life.

chapters.title: Wrapping up
chapters.text:
In the lifelong search for why we exist, the Enlightenment offers the only answer that holds up. We are here to flourish. We exist to reason, to fight the constant pull of entropy, and to build a livable world with science. We exist to evolve and adapt to what we face, to gather information and pass it to the next generation, and in everything we do, to care for humanity and try to make life better for everyone.

We should learn to be grateful for our progress, no matter how much is still undone. That gratitude keeps us optimistic and moving forward. It has taken generations to win equal rights in gender, race, and sexuality, but much remains to be done. We should focus on the good, look at how far we have come, and draw strength from it to keep pushing. As long as reason, science, and humanism work together to guide us, we can trust that we are on the right path. Inequality is still real, but compared with overall human progress, it is not the main story. When it comes to well-being, inequality cannot outweigh health, prosperity, knowledge, safety, and peace. So much good happens around us every day. It is time to be grateful for our progress.

Try this. Grab a notepad, on your phone or laptop. Skip the news for a week. Carry the notepad with you and write down anything good that happens to you or to those around you for seven days.
